speaker: interviewer
text: Well, listen--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I'm trying not to be--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold it. Hold it for the video, because that's-- because it's an interesting-- it's-- it's an interesting part of--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was still the good time, because then it start to get bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is what you'll tell me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ready to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This uncle, my aunt and uncle-- this was my father's sister. She was really wonderful. And I really never knew her. But this-- she tried to help. She knew that she's doing something right, even to the extent of risking their life. But it was such a great help. A whole year, I used to receive packages, little packages. And one interesting thing that the Germans also did for males and females-- I don't know why they need-- they added a middle name. My name, my Biblical name, is Rivka. I'm going by Regina, but it's Rivka. So I had to add to every name, Rivka Sarah. Every boy had to add Israel. I don't know whether this was-- it helped them to know who's Jewish. Like let's say, my husband, Jack, Yakov Israel Levin, or Lewin. Every female was Sarah, on everyone. So when I saw those postcards, there was an address. And I'll tell you how I learned in my head. It's the spelling I used in Polish that I didn't know that there is a different way of saying. So I used to say roo-a for [FRENCH]. [FRENCH] is a street in French. Later on, I learned French. But roo-a gow-harrat. That how I said it, with [THROAT CLEARING]. Like roo-a gow-harrat Brooksell. And I knew that-- and the house number. I don't remember now, but it's on the postcards. And this is what in my mind all the time. And when I wrote them, when the war was over, I realized that they weren't there anymore in that place, because that was where they were hiding. But I found them, which I'll let you know when we get there. During the year, that one year from 1942 to '43, life, it wasn't good because you were a prisoner. But it was easier because we-- we learned to be productive, to do something, to maintain not to get sick. They didn't sort out. They didn't make selections. There was no crematorium. It was a production. I compare it, basically-- except we didn't have husbands, and wives, and children like the Schindler people-- but I compare it in a way, it was a camp where if you could work, you were fine. You could manage to live as long as you didn't get in their way or you didn't act like you against them or lazy. That was our program. Just do what they tell you to do. During the year, there were a lot of non-Jewish people that used to work in those factories. They weren't allowed to come into the factory while this shift was going on. We had a shift, a separate shift, from the resident, from the workers that used to work before the war. But I do remember it was interesting that the previous people, like the previous woman that would operate the machine that I operate, if she would eat an apple, she would deliberately-- she wouldn't leave me the apple, but she would leave the skin of the apple, like she forgot to clean it up. Or she would leave something in a cup. Sort of like-- and I realized what she's trying to do, but she was scared to help. But they did. And I heard from later on, when we went to our barracks, some girls would say, I had a treat today. Somebody left something for me to help. Now, every little bit helped later on, when the-- when times got worse. Because I wasn't as skinny yet. And I'm not being when they did check us. They checked. They disrobed the girls, stand in the line, and see how-- how you look. If you were able to exist, you weren't totally like a stick, like, then you were OK. And in our camp were a lot of young people. Lot of young women. The oldest maybe was in the late 20s, the oldest. And this was-- this production was very precise to them. Because nobody tried not to-- not to behave, just to survive. For the extended, I remember going to work. It was quite a ways. And we had to-- oh, yes. Later on, somebody came up with an in their-- their-- they were worried how to make our life more miserable, or trying to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Forget the shift. We are not going to meet anyone. We're going to work the worst shift in the world, from 3:00 PM till 3:00 AM, or from 3:00 AM to 3:00 PM. Can you believe it? That's not a day and it's not a night. That's the way they want work choice. So working pitch dark. And this is in the mountains. I have this postcard. It's in the mountains. It's cold. It's snowing in the winter. Its in Czechoslovakia, and it's very-- it's beautiful there for vacation, but it's very, very cold. Very-- the air is very sharp there. So working, I remember behind me. I wish I would know where these women are or who they are. Straighten up. Walk straight. Walk straight. Don't give in. Don't give in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Voices. It meant so much. You know, you just drop your bundle. I heard some women, that they-- women, they were still young or maybe a little older than me-- don't drag your feet. Go straight. Walk, walk. You going to be OK. You have to survive. They want in spite. In Polish, I'm trying to translate. But in spite of it, we are going to be OK. And the-- the sound of it was so optimistic, so cheerful, that-- that was terrible important, as hungry, as tired as you were. And there were also supervisors, Jewish supervisors, which were bitches. Women. Ugly women. Women that thought that the world is for them, which were low characters. You scared of people like that. You try to get away from them more than from a German supervisor, because they were-- they were trying to please their superior. And they were spanking girls and yelling. And [GERMAN], go, go, go. Fast, fast. Move, move. Or something. And hit. So I felt, and I said to myself, if I should make it-- and I hope I will-- I'm not going to be the last one or the first one. Because when you walked in and said good morning, they start yelling at you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you say good morning? You're not my friend. And if you didn't say it, say, [GERMAN], I can't even say it in English. You dare think-- you don't even greet me? So we didn't know what to do. Say it, yes or no. Say good morning or don't say good morning. So I-- I tried to go with a stream in the middle, never the end, so that I don't get hit and I don't get hurt. And I was doing-- I was small, and short, and little, and not-- not interfering, not getting. Just trying to manage as much as possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so between my-- my aunt's help and whatever we could do until 1943. When 1943 arrived, they said that this small camp is going to be transferred. That was bad. They took us. Oh, we heard rumors from some of the supervisors that they going to go in training. And they going to assess these women that were wearing uniforms were female soldiers watching the camp, just-- just like doing a job. So they send them to a training where they have to become SS frauen, SS women. With a-- not just swastika, no. They had a whole training. I think they had to wear under the arm some kind of sign that they belong to the Nazi party. They became Nazi party.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we heard from some of the girls, the women that were wearing them, that they cried. One admitted to one of our workers. And the news came around quietly that she joined simply to survive, to be-- to be-- well, a soldier. To have a job. But she was never a Nazi. She was never trying to be a member of a party. They forced her. And those women, one day I remember one girl, one soldier, what, you crying that you lost your parents? You cry that you're away from home? I miss my family, too. A girl in the uniforms. I miss my family. I don't like to be here as much. I would like to be in my home. Who knows from what town in Germany she came? I don't know them. But they send them to watch us. And they came from some other German town, and they never went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, they probably survived, most of them did. Because our camp wasn't-- they weren't literally criminals. They didn't kill or shoot anyone in that camp. So they-- after the war, when the Allies marched in, they gave them a sentence. And I assume after seven, eight years, or 10 years, they were all free to go. I'm sure. I never went back to Germany and I never will. But that-- that was the idea, I think, because they didn't-- just joined the Nazi party. And I think they were forced to do that. So at that time, when they were training them, they took us, like 1,200 of us, into a larger camp which belonged to the same company where I worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that camp have a special name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was Ober Altstadt, which would translate in English, Old City. Ober Altstadt. That's a little town. It's not far from Prague. It belongs now to Czechoslovakia. Because I never was-- yes, I was in Prague on the way with the Red Cross-- but we didn't stop-- on the way to Poland when we were liberated. We had to go through Prague. Prague is, I think, the capital of Czechoslovakia. But this town, that was already tough. Because we weren't alone. We joined some old timers. And you know, you're probably familiar, you've probably heard it from people, once a camp, just like a prison, gets new inmates, they're the victims. They're the worst. They get the worst work. They get the worst barracks. They were pushed around. And it wasn't the same. So we tried to hold on to the ones, like I had the barracks, I had the-- the bunk where we slept, like bunks all over that room, with other women that I knew from the beginning, from 1942. Because when we came in '42, there was clean stuff. Not sheets, but there were blankets. There were pillows. It was clean. It was a brand new place. And here, we came to a camp which was used, and was already. But the same work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they assigned us. Right away, they knew my number. So and so, you're assigned to this and this fabric factory. And you know what your job is, you know what you're doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: No more mail. Nothing. And in that camp, we had a man which was a maniac. I think he was-- either he hated women, either he was some kind of a-- a pervert. He hated women with a passion. He looked at us and spit. He looked at us and was trying to think the worst idea after a day's work, and production was doing OK. We did all the things. He would find an idea what to do. This, I'll never forget. It was pouring. It was winter. This is in the mountains. And like we had this week here, some of the gutters were full with water. Some of the water was standing. So he took buckets, lined up all the women, and asked to empty the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine? This was such an unnecessary-- it was crazy. But he was sitting there with a-- with a whip, like a whip, smiling, enjoying it. And each one, tired and hungry, not enough clothes, not enough coverage, or shoes, or underwear.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this-- this was-- I can still see the face, some of the girls fainted. Some of them fell in the-- in the mud. You know, like a line up to carry a bucket of water, empty it. Carry a bucket. And this was going on for hours. We had a terrible shift. We came home from work on that for hours. And then they tried to punish people. All kinds of ideas that they were up to those place. This was already hard. And there was no help, no medical help, nothing. This was the worst part. Every one of us that we knew each other tried not to get sick. Because once we went into this section which was like what they call the [GERMAN], the building of the sick, typhoid, you never came out. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how-- how do you protect yourself? What you do? But I don't know. I still don't understand. Now, my daughters, anybody catches a cold. I'm talking about three years without hardly any clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another thing which we didn't, we didn't menstruate. I don't know what we did. Most of the girls were amazed. I was just beginning to be a girl. I was just-- there was no-- no cycle. Nothing. We were checked after the war, right after the war. Thank God I have two daughters. But I mean, that was strange. None of the women had their period in that time. So I don't know. Some people say they put something in the food. I think malnutrition can do it, definitely, when you not enough vitamins. And people were losing sight. People were losing teeth. Thank God, I'm an old lady and I still-- I'm still OK. But it also may be because I did have some help between. You know, occasionally, we got-- we got to share something. Like if I managed to get something, we shared. And if you cooperated with the girls and one helped another as much as we could. And you're young. When you're young-- I mean, a 50, 60 year old can never make it like that for three years in such conditions. And then '43, '44, we started to hear rumors that the war is going to come to an end. There were bombings in between. There were, what do you call when we went and they were building under the factories--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bomb shelters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bomb shelters. Bomb shelters. And we were happy. We were back to work. And we were laughing and singing. Nobody was afraid. Nobody. And thank God, the Allies were cruising around. They didn't throw the bombs. Apparently, they knew those are concentration camps. So there was-- but there was a lot of rumors that the Allies are close, and-- and there was bombing. And we wanted to go. We wanted to go. I remember girls committing suicide. Couldn't take it anymore. There were some that were beaten badly. They were sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think about committing suicide?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Such-- I wanted to see my family again. I said, I can't do it. And what I believed in my heart, always till the last minute, if I'm in camp, they're home. They must be alive. They-- they wrote to me. I have proof. They're OK. I was not for a moment believing that they wiped out the whole town. And then that was the end. I never. And I must tell you, optimism. Believe in something, can keep you going at night. And-- and some people were-- you stupid. You're an idiot. You're an idiot. I don't want to hear it. I don't want to hear it. I don't want to believe that I'm such an idiot. That I don't-- I will never see-- my mother was only 39 years old. So I figured-- also, if she would have left my father, she would have gone with me. Because I, in my camp-- like I said, this wasn't an extermination. There were mothers and daughters working together. As long as you did what-- what the factory work. I remember she like waved to me. She didn't leave my father. They both went. The only thing I would like to know-- I never will-- if they suffered long. [SOBBING] If they took the-- If they burned them, or if they gassed them, or whatever, how long they suffered. If they had a-- a fast-- it always-- this one thing always stings. But you know, when I was liberated, I wasn't as emotional as-- as I got older. Everybody was an orphan. Everybody was liberated. We were all young people. We were all starting a new life. I mean, it wasn't a novelty. I wasn't the only one. I Wasn't an orphan, here I am alone. We were hundreds of us. We came all back together. I didn't think about it. I didn't think. I didn't even mourn. I didn't mourn enough because I didn't think about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I convinced myself, they're not dead. It can't be. Can't be. And that's the way I-- I lived till 1943. But in 1944, when the rumors spread that the Germans came back from Russia defeated, that the Allies started to approach, we used to go, come on, already. When are you going to come? We were praying that the Allies get into the war and come.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the beginning, '45, when Auschwitz was already liberated, we heard rumors that male camps are being evacuated. And we saw transports of skeletons walking past. But we weren't allowed-- we couldn't-- we couldn't even approach. I saw-- I remember seeing transports going, going. And we were still there. But almost towards the end of the war, if I-- if I have something else to add, I don't think so. But if I remember almost towards the end of the war, this lady in charge, she was called the [GERMAN]. She was wearing a uniform. Frau Hoffman, such a hefty German woman. She came over, made an appeal. Gathered us all outside. And she said, we have news for you. She started to prepare us. We going to-- The war will end. She didn't say the Germans will lose. The war will end. And when the Allies march in, whoever is coming, tell them that we didn't-- we weren't bad to you, or something. A whole speech. And they started to bring out from storage a little bit of clothing, decent clothing. She gave us threads, and needles, put a button. Don't look shabby. They tried to make up because they knew, they knew that Hitler's dead. We heard that Hitler's dead. We didn't know if it's true. But we heard months before that Hitler is gone. He's dead. So she started to speak in a nice way. And she didn't call us ugly names anymore. But she used to say, women, Juden, frau Juden, women Jews, listen to me carefully. We are getting towards the end of the war, and we weren't killing anyone-- which they didn't, really. But they beat up and made all kinds of miserable things. We want you-- we want put in a good words for us. Everybody say, oh yeah, oh yeah. But then when the first Russian tank broke the gates and the barbed wire, when they walked in, girls went crazy. They really went. They jumped on top of the soldiers. They jumped on the tanks. And then they started to take revenge. They wanted to take revenge, not to beat them-- because by the way, they didn't let us close to those women. They were gathering the-- the supervisors, all these women in the uniforms, German woman. They didn't let us touch them. They were afraid that maybe we would really go crazy after all these years. So they didn't. But some women were ripping. I have one little souvenir that a girl gave me that she pulled from a belt from one of the lady soldiers, supervisor. And I'll show it to you later on. It's a medallion that she belonged to a ski organization in the mountains, because this was in the mountains. That she was some kind of-- on a program, or member of a ski. So it's like medallion which is more than 50 years old. And I kept it. She-- the girl gave it to me. Says, here this is at least something from your-- and in that book, I have a sticker, which was-- this was kind of money, supposed to be pay that they paid us for our work. It's like-- I'll show it to you-- like a little ID of some kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day of the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. May 8, 1945. May 8, 1945. Two years later, in 1947, I married Jack. I married exactly to the day of my liberation. This May 8 will be 50 years. This was actually the end of Second World War. This was the day when the war ended. And we were liberated. And right after that, when the-- the liberation came, you know, people couldn't control themselves. They were running around like crazy. They were trying to find food, trying to find clothes. So the-- the Russian soldiers opened up some storages in this town, which was very dangerous. Because it was like a stampede. A literally a stampede. People started to step one on the-- it was a human stampede. I'll never forget it. People were running. If somebody fell, they'll step over him. They'll kick him. People were really not-- I felt, I kept on saying to myself, we managed three years. Let's not-- let's not go crazy now. Let's try to control our feelings. But I do remember that-- that run. That it's-- they were running five pairs of shoes, six. Shoes, clothing. Not even if it was fitting you. It's like grabbing things, a stampede of human bodies from-- from joy, from the liberation of opening up the gates.

speaker: interviewer
text: How we doing now? What--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm at 26 minutes, 27 minutes now. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe this is a good time to just--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We can stop.
